---
title: Working with data
---

## Add some ready-made data

## Add data from local storage

## Re-ordering data and creating groups

Re-ordering Layers: The "Stacking" Logic
QGIS renders data from the bottom up. This means layers at the top of the list will cover layers underneath them.
•	How to move them: Simply click and drag a layer up or down in the list.
•	The Golden Rule of Stacking: To keep everything visible, follow this standard order:
1.	Top: Points (small icons, easily covered).
2.	Middle: Lines (roads, rivers).
3.	Bottom: Polygons (land parcels, lakes).
4.	Background: Rasters (satellite imagery, hillshades).

2. Creating Groups: Bundling Your Data
Groups allow you to collapse multiple layers into a single folder, keeping your workspace clean.
•	To create a group: Right-click anywhere in the Layers Panel and select Add Group. You can then drag existing layers into it.
•	Batch Visibility: Clicking the checkbox next to a Group name will turn all layers inside that group on or off at once. This is perfect for toggling between different "themes" (e.g., "Environmental Data" vs. "Infrastructure").
•	Nesting: You can even create groups inside groups for complex projects with dozens of layers.

3. Mutually Exclusive Groups (The "Radio Button" Trick)
If you have multiple base maps (like Google Satellite and OpenStreetMap) and only want one visible at a time:
1.	Put them in a group together.
2.	Right-click the Group and select Mutually Exclusive Group.
3.	Now, when you turn one layer on, the others in that group will automatically turn off.
4. Renaming for Clarity

Downloaded data often has cryptic names like TL_2023_06_ADDR.shp.
•	Slow Double-Click (or press F2) on the layer name to rename it to something human-readable, like "City Addresses." This only changes the display name in QGIS, not the actual file on your hard drive.


## Theory - Vectors and Rasters

Vector vs. Raster: Key Differences
Vector data uses math to represent individual objects, while Raster data uses a grid of pixels to represent a continuous area.

Choose Vector when precision and specific location data are key. It is the standard for urban planning, navigation, and boundary mapping.

Choose Raster for broad environmental analysis. It is the best choice for satellite imagery, terrain modeling, and scientific modeling (like predicting floods).

In a real QGIS project, you will almost always use both together. For example, you might place a Vector layer of site locations on top of a Raster satellite image to see how they align with the terrain.

![The difference between Vector and Rasters](../figs/GISI/Working_With_Files/Raster_Vector.png)
